target.name: Indole
target.motivation: Indoles are privileged structural motifs in N-heterocyclic chemistry, serving as pivotal building blocks for natural products, agrochemicals, and bioactive pharmaceuticals. The development of a general and facile platform capable of directly transforming commodity chemicals into multi-functionalized indoles persists as an unmet challenge.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Fischer indole synthesis using aryl hydrazines and carbonyl compounds followed by acid-mediated [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement under thermal conditions.
Catalytic protocol for modular synthesis of C2-CF3CH2-substituted indole derivatives with non-activated olefins, aryldiazonium salts, and sodium triflate.
Photocatalysis to construct trifluoromethylated azo intermediates followed by Brønsted acid-catalyzed cyclization to access indole derivatives.

success_criteria.free_text: Broad substrate scope, excellent functional group tolerance, mild conditions, and scalability for industrial application.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C and C-N bonds forming the indole ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: The proposed strategy leverages a photo-driven bifunctional iron-catalyzed system to enable C(sp3)-H activation or decarboxylation, followed by a sigmatropic rearrangement cyclization. This approach aims to overcome limitations of traditional methods by using readily available arylamines and alkanes/carboxylic acids as starting materials, avoiding prefunctionalization and harsh conditions.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy integrates iron-based photocatalysis via a ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) pathway for C–H activation or decarboxylation, and Lewis acid catalysis for the subsequent cascade cyclization. This 'Swiss-Army-knife-type' system aims to provide a direct, one-pot route to indoles from commodity chemicals, addressing issues of narrow substrate scope and multi-step syntheses in prior methods.

thought.background: Previous work by the group demonstrated direct fusion of iron photocatalysis with traditional iron redox catalysis for alkane C–H activation and stereoselective olefin construction. Iron catalysts are known for versatile catalytic capabilities including redox and Lewis acid catalysis, and emerging photocatalytic competence.
thought.gap: Existing indole synthesis methods suffer from laborious multi-step protocols, narrow substrate scope, and harsh conditions. A unified catalytic system integrating multiple iron functionalities for indole synthesis is needed.
thought.decision: To address the challenges in indole synthesis, we hypothesize a 'Swiss-Army-knife-type' catalytic platform integrating iron photocatalysis and iron Lewis acid catalysis. This system is proposed to simultaneously leverage in situ diazotization of anilines, LMCT-driven C–H activation/decarboxylation, and Lewis acid-catalyzed cascade cyclization, expecting a streamlined route to various indoles from industrial feedstocks.

evidence.text_span: Building upon this foundation, we next attempt to design a Swiss-Army-knife-type catalytic platform integrating iron photocatalysis and iron LA catalysis for addressing the mentioned critical challenges—notably laborious multistep protocols and narrow substrate scope—in indole synthesis (Fig. 1c). This multifunctional system is proposed to simultaneously leverage three consecutive units, including in situ diazotization of anilines, LMCT-driven C–H activation/decarboxylation, and LAcatalyzed cascade cyclization. By orchestrating these mechanistically distinct pathways within one pot, we expect to establish an integrated and streamlined route to various indoles directly from industrial feedstocks.

thought.background: A photo-driven bifunctional iron-catalyzed strategy is proposed for one-pot indole synthesis.
thought.gap: The feasibility of one-step indole synthesis through photo-induced LMCT-mediated alkane activation needs to be explored.
thought.decision: To explore the feasibility, we used cyclohexane (1a) and 4-methoxyaniline (1b) as model substrates with FeCl3·6H2O (0.1 equiv.) as catalyst, tBuONO (1 equiv.), and HBF4 (1.0 equiv.) as diazotization reagents, and MeCN as solvent under N2 and irradiation of 30 W 405 nm LEDs for 12 h, expecting the desired indole product.

outcome.observation: The desired product 1c was obtained in 85% yield.

evidence.text_span: Initially, we explored the feasibility of one-step indole synthesis through photo-induced LMCT-mediated alkane activation, utilizing cyclohexane (1a) and 4-methoxyaniline (1b) as model substrates (Table 1, more details see supplementary Table S1). We were delighted to find that the reaction obtained the desired product 1c in 85% yield with FeCl3·6H2O (0.1 equiv.) as catalyst, tBuONO (1 equiv.), and HBF4 (1.0 equiv.) as diazotization reagents, and MeCN as solvent under N2 and irradiation of 30 W 405 nm LEDs for 12 h (entry 1).

thought.background: The initial reaction yielded 85% of the desired product 1c using 1.0 equiv. of HBF4.
thought.gap: The influence of the amount of HBF4 on the yield needs to be systematically examined to optimize the reaction conditions.
thought.decision: To optimize the yield, we systematically examined the influence of the amount of HBF4, testing different equivalents (0.9, 0.8, 0.75, 0.7, 0.6 equiv.), expecting to find an optimal concentration for higher yield.

outcome.observation: HBF4 (0.7 equiv.) was found to be optimal, affording an excellent isolated yield of 95%.

evidence.text_span: The influence of the amount of HBF4 and other protic acids on the yield was systematically examined (entries 1–10), revealing that HBF4 (0.7 equiv.) was optimal with an excellent isolated yield of 95% (entry 5).

thought.background: FeCl3·6H2O is an effective catalyst for the reaction.
thought.gap: Other iron catalysts and Lewis acid catalysts need to be evaluated to determine their activity.
thought.decision: To explore catalyst scope, we investigated Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeBr3, and CuCl2 under the optimized conditions, expecting to find alternative active catalysts.

outcome.observation: Fe2(SO4)3·xH2O failed to produce indole (N.D.).

evidence.text_span: Other iron catalysts were investigated but failed to produce indole without chlorides (entries 12–14). Correspondingly, the incorporation of an exogenous chloride source in conjunction with Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was observed to be effective (entry 15), suggesting the chlorine species to be an essential additive for alkane activation. Furthermore, a copper catalyst CuCl2 (entry 16), which has been reported to have similar photocatalytic activity to FeCl352, and a variety of common Lewis acid catalysts (details see Table S1) were evaluated; however, none produced the desired product under the reaction conditions, demonstrating that the iron catalyst exhibits more than one catalytic property in this system.

thought.background: Fe(NO3)3·9H2O and other iron catalysts without chlorides failed to produce indole.
thought.gap: The role of chloride species in alkane activation needs to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To confirm the importance of chloride, we added an exogenous chloride source (TEAC) in conjunction with Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, expecting to observe product formation.

outcome.observation: The incorporation of an exogenous chloride source (TEAC) with Fe(NO3)3·9H2O was effective, yielding 56% of the product, suggesting chlorine species are essential for alkane activation.

evidence.text_span: Other iron catalysts were investigated but failed to produce indole without chlorides (entries 12–14). thought.background: MeCN is an effective solvent for the reaction.
thought.gap: The influence of different solvents on the reaction yield needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To optimize the solvent, we screened various solvents including DMSO, DCE, and DMF, expecting to find a solvent that provides comparable or better yields than MeCN.

outcome.observation: DMSO failed to initiate the reaction (N.D.).

evidence.text_span: The solvent screening revealed that different solvent choices significantly impacted reaction yields, with DMSO, DCE, and DMF failing to initiate the reaction (entry 17, details see Table S1). Meanwhile, acetone proved to be suitable for this transformation but afforded lower yields than MeCN (entry 18).

outcome.observation: Acetone proved to be suitable for this transformation but afforded lower yields (65%) than MeCN.

thought.background: 405 nm LEDs provided the optimal yield of 95%.
thought.gap: The influence of different light sources (wavelengths) on the reaction efficiency needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To optimize the light source, we tested various light sources with different wavelengths, including 365 nm, 395 nm, 455 nm, and CFL, expecting to find a light source that provides comparable or better efficiency than 405 nm LEDs.

outcome.observation: 365 nm LEDs resulted in lower efficiency (67% yield) compared to 405 nm LEDs.

evidence.text_span: Various light sources with different wavelengths, including 365 nm, 395 nm, 455 nm, and CFL, were tested next. The results indicated that the reaction efficiency was not as optimal as that achieved with 405 nm LEDs (entries 19–22).

thought.background: The reaction proceeds efficiently with FeCl3·6H2O as catalyst and 405 nm LED irradiation.
thought.gap: The necessity of the iron catalyst and light irradiation needs to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To confirm the essential roles of the iron catalyst and light, we performed control experiments in the absence of the iron catalyst or light, expecting no product formation.

outcome.observation: No product was observed in the absence of an iron catalyst (N.D.).

evidence.text_span: Control experiments revealed that no product was observed in the absence of an iron catalyst (entry 25), light (entry 26), or under air (entry 27).

outcome.observation: No product was observed under air (N.D.).

evidence.text_span: Control experiments revealed that no product was observed in the absence of an iron catalyst (entry 25), light (entry 26), or under air (entry 27).

thought.background: Optimal conditions for one-step indole synthesis from alkanes and arylamines have been established.
thought.gap: The substrate scope for various alkanes and arylamines needs to be systematically investigated.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the broad applicability of the method, we investigated the reaction scope using diverse alkane and arylamine substrates, including cycloalkanes (5-, 6-, 7-, 8-, and 12-membered rings) and linear n-pentane, expecting consistently excellent reactivity and functional group tolerance.

outcome.observation: Cyclohexane (1a) and 4-methoxyaniline (1b) yielded 1c in 95%. Cycloalkanes (1c–6c) demonstrated consistently excellent reactivity (>90% yields). Linear n-pentane yielded only 2° C–H activation products (7c).

evidence.text_span: With the optimal reaction conditions for the one-step iron-catalyzed construction of indole skeletons in hand, we proceeded to systematically investigate the reaction scope using diverse alkane and arylamine substrates (Fig. 2). Preliminary investigations employing a series of cycloalkanes (5-, 6-, 7-, 8-, and 12-membered rings; 1c–6c) demonstrated consistently excellent reactivity (>90% yields). Notably, cyclooctane (5c) exhibited extraordinary reactivity, and its derived indole product constitutes a pivotal synthetic intermediate for Iprindole, a finding that significantly streamlines the preparation of this pharmacologically important scaffold. When we applied a linear n-pentane instead, only 2° C–H activation products were acquired with no 1° C–H activation observed in this system (7c).

thought.background: The one-step indole synthesis from alkanes and arylamines showed broad scope for cycloalkanes and linear alkanes.
thought.gap: The substrate scope for various aniline derivatives needs to be investigated to confirm functional group tolerance.
thought.decision: To further evaluate the method's versatility, we investigated the substrate scope with various aniline derivatives, including those with electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups, expecting good to excellent yields and remarkable functional group tolerance.

outcome.observation: Structurally diverse substituted anilines reacted efficiently with cyclohexane to construct indole frameworks in good to excellent yields (51-95%, 8c–50c). The transformation showed remarkable functional group tolerance, accommodating both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents with high efficiency.

evidence.text_span: We next investigated the substrate scope of this multi-step cyclization reaction with various aniline derivatives. The experimental results demonstrated that structurally diverse substituted anilines could efficiently react with cyclohexane to construct indole frameworks in good to excellent yields (51-95%, 8c–50c). The transformation showed remarkable functional group tolerance, accommodating both electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents with high efficiency. Firstly, anilines with electron-donating groups including alkyls (Me, Et, tBu, iPr, 8c–11c) and ethers (OEt, OPh, OCF3, 12c–14c) performed particularly well (almost around 90%). Moreover, the reaction also exhibited good to excellent compatibility towards various electron-withdrawing groups, including halogens (F, Cl, Br, I, 18c–21c), trifluoromethyl (22c), cyano (24c), esters (COOMe, COOEt, 24c, 25c), carbonyls (Ac, COPh, 26c, 27c), and sulfonyl derivatives (SO2Me, SO2NH2, 28c, 29c). Remarkably, hydroxyl-containing moieties, including phenolic (81% 16c) and aliphatic alcohol groups (87%, 15c), and drug-relevant sulfamide units (61%, 29c) remain intact without protective strategies, providing a wide chemical space for further diversification. Following steric evaluation revealed that meta-substituted anilines (30c–34c) afforded slightly lower yields (59-73%) relative to their para-substituted counterparts. Multi-substituted substrates performed well regardless of electronic properties, with both mixed electron-donating/withdrawing combinations (35c, 36c, 38c, 41c) and biselectron-withdrawing systems (37c, 39c, 40c, 42c, 43c) maintaining moderate to good reaction efficiency. Notably, the indole product (48c, 52%) obtained from the reaction of a free carboxylic acid-containing aniline with cycloheptane serves as a key intermediate for A-FABP inhibitors, demonstrating the practical utility of this transformation in medicinal chemistry. Heteroaromatic amines involving quinoline and benzofuran motifs were also compatible, affording the corresponding indole product in moderate yields (49c, 50c).

thought.background: The one-step indole synthesis method demonstrated broad substrate scope and functional group tolerance at small scale.
thought.gap: The scalability and robustness of the methodology for industrial manufacturing applications need to be evaluated.
thought.decision: To demonstrate industrial potential, we executed a gram-scale synthesis using 4-methoxyaniline 1b (10.0 g, 81.2 mmol), expecting no obvious efficiency loss and confirming robustness for manufacturing.

outcome.observation: A gram-scale synthesis of 1c was achieved in 91.9% isolated yield with no obvious efficiency loss. A continuous-flow photocatalytic micro-reactor also afforded similar results with reduced time and attenuated light irradiation.

evidence.text_span: Moreover, we executed a gram-scale synthesis employing 4-methoxyaniline 1b (10.0 g, 81.2 mmol), achieving product 1c in 91.9% isolated yield with no obvious efficiency loss (more details see Fig. S12). To further increase the light utilization efficiency, a well-designed continuous-flow photocatalytic micro-reactor was used, capable to afford similar results to those of the batch reaction with a reduced time and attenuated light irradiation (more details see Fig. S13). This successful scale-up and flow synthesis not only confirms the robustness of our methodology but also highlights its potential for industrial manufacturing applications.

thought.background: The iron-catalyzed photoredox one-step coupling of alkanes with arylamines has been achieved, but limitations include difficult activation of primary C–H bonds and exclusive formation of 2,3-disubstituted indoles. Carboxylic acids are versatile radical precursors.
thought.gap: The application scope of this bifunctional iron-catalyzed system needs to be expanded to include carboxylic acids as substrates to overcome the limitations of alkane activation.
thought.decision: To expand the application scope, we selected the reaction between cyclohexanecarboxylic acid (1a′) and 4-methoxybenzenediazonium tetrafluoroborate (1b′) as the model system for condition optimization of the decarboxylative indole construction process, expecting to establish optimal conditions for this new substrate class.

outcome.observation: The combination of FeCl3·6H2O (0.1 equiv.), di-(2-picolyl)amine L1 (0.2 equiv.), DABCO (1 equiv.), and KHSO4 (5 equiv.) in MeCN under 30 W 405 nm LED irradiation provided the target indole product 1c in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: At the outset of our investigation, we selected the reaction between cyclohexanecarboxylic acid (1a′) and 4-methoxybenzenediazonium tetrafluoroborate (1b′) as the model system for condition optimization of the decarboxylative indole construction process (more details see Supplementary Tables S2 and S3). After systematic investigation of catalysts, ligands, additives, and solvents, we successfully established the following optimal reaction conditions. The combination of FeCl3·6H2O (0.1 equiv.) as an inexpensive photocatalyst as well as a Lewis-acid catalyst, di-(2-picolyl)amine L1 (0.2 equiv.) as the ligand, and DABCO (1 equiv.) and KHSO4 (5 equiv.) as additives in MeCN under 30 W 405 nm LED irradiation provided the target indole product 1c in 88% yield (Table S2, entry 15).

thought.background: FeCl3·6H2O is an effective catalyst for decarboxylative indole construction.
thought.gap: Other iron catalysts need to be evaluated for their activity in this system.
thought.decision: To explore catalyst scope, we investigated various iron catalysts including FeBr3, Fe(OTf)3, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, and Fe(acac)3, expecting to find alternative active catalysts.

outcome.observation: FeBr3 showed limited activity.

evidence.text_span: Various iron catalysts, including FeBr3, Fe(OTf)3, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, and Fe(acac)3, all showed limited activity (entries 35–42).

thought.gap: Other metal chlorides need to be evaluated for their activity in this system.
thought.decision: To explore catalyst scope, we investigated other metal chlorides, including ZrCl4 and CuCl2, expecting to find alternative active catalysts.

outcome.observation: ZrCl4 presented no activity.

evidence.text_span: Interestingly, CeCl3 exhibited moderate catalytic efficiency (67% yield, entry 44), while other metal chlorides, including ZrCl4 and CuCl2, presented no activity (entries 45−48).

outcome.observation: CeCl3 exhibited moderate catalytic efficiency (67% yield).

thought.background: Optimal conditions for decarboxylative indole construction using pre-formed diazonium salt have been established.
thought.gap: The efficiency of a direct one-pot coupling between arylamines and carboxylic acids needs to be evaluated.
thought.decision: To simplify the process, we attempted initial one-step studies for direct one-pot coupling between arylamines and carboxylic acids, expecting to achieve reasonable efficiency.

outcome.observation: Initial one-step studies revealed limited efficiency, yielding only 54% even after extensive condition optimization.

evidence.text_span: Building upon our target to establish a Swiss-Army-knife-type catalytic platform for one-pot synthesis of indoles, we further sought to expand this methodology to a direct one-pot coupling between arylamines and carboxylic acids (Tables S4 and S5). Initial one-step studies revealed limited efficiency, with the transformation yielding only 54% even after extensive condition optimization (Table S4, entry 3).

thought.background: Initial one-step studies for direct one-pot coupling between arylamines and carboxylic acids showed limited efficiency (54% yield).
thought.gap: An improved strategy is needed to enhance the yield of the one-pot coupling.
thought.decision: To improve the yield, we devised an optimized one-pot two-step strategy involving an initial arylamine diazotization at 0 °C and a following iron-catalyzed decarboxylative cyclization in situ without intermediate purification, expecting a significantly promoted yield.

outcome.observation: This optimized one-pot two-step protocol significantly promoted the yield of target indole product 1c to 86%.

evidence.text_span: To address this issue, we devised an improved one-pot two-step strategy involving an initial arylamine diazotization at 0 °C and a following iron-catalyzed decarboxylative cyclization in situ without intermediate purification. This optimized protocol significantly promoted the yield of target indole product 1c to 86% (Table S5, entry 2).

thought.background: An optimized one-pot two-step protocol for indole synthesis from arylamines and carboxylic acids has been established.
thought.gap: The reaction scope using various carboxylic acids needs to be evaluated, focusing on secondary carboxylic acids and their functional group tolerance.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the versatility of the method, we investigated the reaction scope using various carboxylic acids, starting with secondary carboxylic acids including cyclic and linear variants, expecting effective transformation and good yields.

outcome.observation: Cyclic secondary carboxylic acids (60c–65c) and carbocyclic acids of varying ring sizes (1c, 66c, 67c) were effective substrates, affording corresponding indole products in 68–87% yields. Linear secondary carboxylic acids (71c–77c) and phenyl-substituted analogs (78c–82c) also efficiently delivered indole frameworks in 74% yields.

evidence.text_span: With the optimized one-pot two-step protocol established, we next evaluated the reaction scope using various carboxylic acids and arylamines (Fig. 3). The investigation first focused on secondary carboxylic acids. Cyclic substrates involving substituted cyclohexanecarboxylic acids (bearing methyl, propyl, ester, hydroxy, amide, and fluoro groups; 60c–65c) and carbocyclic acids of varying ring sizes (5- to 7-membered rings; 1c, 66c, 67c) were all proved to be effective substrates, affording the corresponding indole products in 68–87% yields. Notably, with 1-methylpiperidine-4-carboxylic acid as a substrate, our optimized decarboxylation protocol enabled the robust and efficient construction of a pharmacologically valuable piperidine-fused indole scaffold (68c–70c). This privileged scaffold serves as a versatile synthetic intermediate and has been strategically employed in the concise synthesis of biologically active compounds, such as Dimebolin (69c) and Mebhydrolin (70c). Similarly, a series of secondary carboxylic acids with varying carbon chain lengths (71c–77c, C4–C9) and their phenyl-substituted analogs (78c–82c) efficiently delivered indole frameworks in 74% yields. Subsequent investigation of phenylpropanoic acid substrates demonstrated that aromatic analogs bearing diverse substituents (OMe, Cl, Br, I, CN) could be transformed into corresponding indole derivatives (83c–89c) in good yields.

thought.background: The one-pot two-step protocol works well for secondary carboxylic acids.
thought.gap: Primary carboxylic acids show lower reactivity compared to secondary counterparts, and their efficiency needs to be improved.
thought.decision: To overcome the obstacle of lower reactivity for primary carboxylic acids, we attempted to develop an optimized protocol by modulating catalyst and additive loading and adjusting the irradiation wavelength to 365 nm, expecting to improve the reaction efficiency.

outcome.observation: Comparative studies revealed a little lower reactivity of general primary carboxylic acids relative to their secondary counterparts, with an initial yield of 21%.

evidence.text_span: Nevertheless, comparative studies revealed a little lower reactivity of general primary carboxylic acids relative to their secondary counterparts. To overcome this obstacle, we developed an optimized protocol (details see Table S6) by modulating the catalyst and additive loading and adjusting the irradiation wavelength to 365 nm. Through condition screening, the reaction efficiency using primary carboxylic acids as the substrate was improved by three times (21% 61%).

thought.background: Primary carboxylic acids showed lower reactivity (21% yield) with the initial one-pot two-step protocol.
thought.gap: The reaction efficiency for primary carboxylic acids needs to be significantly improved.
thought.decision: To improve the efficiency, we developed an optimized protocol by modulating the catalyst and additive loading (20 mol% FeCl3·6H2O, 1.5 equiv. DABCO) and adjusting the irradiation wavelength to 365 nm, expecting a substantial increase in yield.

outcome.observation: Through condition screening, the reaction efficiency using primary carboxylic acids as the substrate was improved by three times (from 21% to 61%).

evidence.text_span: Nevertheless, comparative studies revealed a little lower reactivity of general primary carboxylic acids relative to their secondary counterparts. To overcome this obstacle, we developed an optimized protocol (details see Table S6) by modulating the catalyst and additive loading and adjusting the irradiation wavelength to 365 nm. Through condition screening, the reaction efficiency using primary carboxylic acids as the substrate was improved by three times (21% 61%).

thought.background: An optimized protocol for primary carboxylic acids has been developed, improving efficiency to 61%.
thought.gap: The substrate scope for linear primary carboxylic acids with different carbon chain lengths and other functionalized propionic acid derivatives needs to be evaluated.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the broad applicability, we investigated linear primary carboxylic acids (C4–C15) and propionic acid derivatives bearing diverse functional groups, expecting effective participation and satisfactory yields.

outcome.observation: Linear primary carboxylic acids with different carbon chain lengths (C4–C15) all participated effectively, affording C3-substituted indole derivatives (93c–104c) in moderate yields. Propionic acid derivatives with various functional groups (91c, 92c, 105c–111c) were also converted to corresponding indole derivatives in satisfactory yields.

evidence.text_span: Depending on these efforts, linear primary carboxylic acids with different carbon chain lengths (C4–C15) all participated effectively in the reaction, affording C3-substituted indole derivatives (93c–104c) in moderate yields. As the alkyl chain length increased (>C11), the yields showed a slight decrease due to the markedly reduced solubility of acid substrates in MeCN solvent. More propionic acid derivatives bearing diverse functional groups, including alkyl (91c, 92c), halogens (105c, 106c), esters (107c–109c), thioesters (110c), and amides (111c), were also converted to the corresponding indole derivatives in satisfactory yields, showing the excellent functional group compatibility of this method.

thought.background: The one-pot two-step methodology demonstrated excellent functional group compatibility with various carboxylic acids.
thought.gap: The application of this methodology to structurally diverse natural products and bioactive pharmaceutical compounds needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To evaluate synthetic utility, we investigated the application to natural fatty acids (Lauric, Myristic, Palmitic, Oleic, Azelaic acid) and primary carboxylic acid derivatives from biologically relevant scaffolds and drugs (Geraniol, Vanillylacetone, Triclosan, Procaine, Estrone), expecting successful cross-conjugation and indole formation.

outcome.observation: The methodology demonstrated remarkable versatility in accommodating various natural fatty acids (113c–117c) and primary carboxylic acid derivatives from biologically relevant scaffolds and drugs (118c–122c), which were efficiently converted to their corresponding indole derivatives.

evidence.text_span: To evaluate the synthetic utility of this one-pot two-step methodology, we then investigated its application to structurally diverse natural products and bioactive pharmaceutical compounds. The reaction demonstrated remarkable versatility in accommodating various natural fatty acids, including saturated (Lauric acid 113c, Myristic acid 114c, and Palmitic acid 115c) and unsaturated (Oleic acid 116c) variants, as well as the therapeutic agent Azelaic acid (117c), all of which were efficiently converted to their corresponding indole derivatives. The methodology was further extended to primary carboxylic acid derivatives derived from biologically relevant scaffolds and drugs, including Geraniol (118c), Vanillylacetone (119c), Triclosan (120c), Procaine (121c), and Estrone (122c), and the results demonstrated the success of the cross-conjugation between these functional molecules with the indole motif under mild conditions and convenient operation.

thought.background: The one-pot two-step decarboxylative indolization protocol has been established and shown to be versatile with various carboxylic acids.
thought.gap: The compatibility of the protocol with various aromatic amines needs to be evaluated, specifically for constructing functionalized cyclohepta[b]indole derivatives.
thought.decision: To evaluate compatibility and construct a molecular library, cycloheptanecarboxylic acid was chosen as a model substrate to react with various aromatic amines, expecting to afford corresponding cyclohepta[b]indoles in moderate to excellent yields.

outcome.observation: Cycloheptanecarboxylic acid exhibited excellent reactivity, efficiently affording corresponding cyclohepta[b]indoles (123c–152c) in moderate to excellent yields (43-84%). The transformation accommodated both para- and ortho-substituted aryl amines with diverse functional groups.

evidence.text_span: The seven-membered ring fused with an indole core is known as cyclohepta[b]indole, a privileged scaffold in medicinal chemistry. Compounds containing this structural motif exhibit diverse pharmacological activities, including: A-FABP inhibitor, LTB4 inhibitor, SIRT1 inhibitor IV, and anti-tubercular agent60. Building upon these significant medicinal properties, cycloheptanecarboxylic acid was chosen as a model substrate to evaluate the compatibility of the decarboxylative indolization protocol with various aromatic amines, and meanwhile, construct an abundant molecular library for the functionalized cyclohepta[b]indole derivatives (Fig. 4). Gratifyingly, cycloheptanecarboxylic acid exhibited excellent reactivity in this bifunctional iron-catalyzed photoredox system, efficiently affording the corresponding cyclohepta[b]indoles (123c–152c) in moderate to excellent yields (43-84%). This transformation exhibited remarkable versatility by readily accommodating both para- and ortho-substituted aryl amines containing diverse functional groups, including alkyl (123c, 124c), ether (125c–129c), phenolic (128c), alcohol (129c), fluoro (130c, 133c), chloro (131c, 134c), bromo (132c, 135c), trifluoromethyl (136c), nitrile (137c), ester (138c–140c), sulfone (141c), sulfonamide (142c) and carbonyl (143c, 144c). A particularly noteworthy example was the successful application to ester-substituted aniline (139c), as the resulting indole derivatives serve as key intermediates for adipocyte fatty acid-binding protein (A-FABP) inhibitors, underscoring the methodology’s medicinal chemistry relevance. The protocol also proved effective for disubstituted arylamines (145c–147c), enabling access to highly substituted indole scaffolds in competitive yields. 8-Aminoquinoline as a heteroaromatic substrate was tried next, furnishing the corresponding indole in 43% yield (148c). A clear electronic effect was observed in this library, with electron-donating groups presenting enhanced reactivity relative to electron-withdrawing substituents. Furthermore, the methodology successfully incorporated other bioactive motifs from natural products and pharmaceuticals with the valuable cyclohepta[b]indole core, including Borneol (149c), Carvacrol (150c), Thymol (151c), and Naproxen (152c), endowing this molecular library with additional potential value for new drug discovery.

thought.background: The Swiss-Army-knife-type catalytic strategy has demonstrated convenience, practicality, and great functional group tolerance.
thought.gap: The strategy needs to be implemented for route improvement in the total synthesis of pharmaceutically valuable indole-containing drugs.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the utility in total synthesis, we applied the strategy to Iprindole, Mebhydrolin, Melatonin, and A-FABP inhibitor, expecting streamlined protocols, enhanced efficiency, and improved yields compared to traditional methods.

outcome.observation: Iprindole (5d) was efficiently synthesized through a streamlined two-step protocol under mild conditions, achieving 75% isolated yield (vs 43% overall yield for the 4-step route).

evidence.text_span: Initially, the antidepressant Iprindole61,62 (5d), traditionally prepared via a four-step sequence under harsh reaction conditions, was efficiently synthesized through our streamlined two-step protocol under mild conditions, achieving 75% isolated yield (vs 43% overall yield for the 4-step route) with significantly enhanced operational efficiency.

thought.background: The Swiss-Army-knife-type catalytic strategy has demonstrated convenience, practicality, and great functional group tolerance.
thought.gap: The strategy needs to be implemented for route improvement in the total synthesis of pharmaceutically valuable indole-containing drugs.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the utility in total synthesis, we applied the strategy to Iprindole, Mebhydrolin, Melatonin, and A-FABP inhibitor, expecting streamlined protocols, enhanced efficiency, and improved yields compared to traditional methods.

outcome.observation: Mebhydrolin (70d) was efficiently synthesized via the one-pot decarboxylation/cyclization protocol, affording the target compound in 56% total yield (vs 47% overall yield after four steps).

evidence.text_span: Mebhydrolin63,64 (70d), a clinically used antihistamine for histamine-mediated allergic diseases, was efficiently synthesized via our one-pot decarboxylation/cyclization protocol using commercially available aniline and 1-methylpiperidine-4-carboxylic acid as starting materials. This optimized approach afforded the target compound in 56% total yield, representing a substantial improvement over the conventional multistep synthesis (47% overall yield after four steps) and simultaneously enhancing both operational safety and atom economy.

thought.background: The Swiss-Army-knife-type catalytic strategy has demonstrated convenience, practicality, and great functional group tolerance.
thought.gap: The strategy needs to be implemented for route improvement in the total synthesis of pharmaceutically valuable indole-containing drugs.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the utility in total synthesis, we applied the strategy to Iprindole, Mebhydrolin, Melatonin, and A-FABP inhibitor, expecting streamlined protocols, enhanced efficiency, and improved yields compared to traditional methods.

outcome.observation: Melatonin (112c) was efficiently constructed in just one step from readily available starting materials, achieving a yield of 57% with notably improved synthetic efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

evidence.text_span: Melatonin65,66 (112c), as an important endogenous compound with multiple biological activities, has been scientifically demonstrated to possess various pharmacological effects, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, energy metabolism regulation, skin barrier protection, and glucose metabolism improvement. Although several synthetic routes have been developed for industrial production, these methods are generally limited by their multi-step procedures and toxic reagent usage. In our work, the target molecule was efficiently constructed in just one step from readily available starting materials, achieving a yield of 57% with notably improved synthetic efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

thought.background: The Swiss-Army-knife-type catalytic strategy has demonstrated convenience, practicality, and great functional group tolerance.
thought.gap: The strategy needs to be implemented for route improvement in the total synthesis of pharmaceutically valuable indole-containing drugs.
thought.decision: To demonstrate the utility in total synthesis, we applied the strategy to Iprindole, Mebhydrolin, Melatonin, and A-FABP inhibitor, expecting streamlined protocols, enhanced efficiency, and improved yields compared to traditional methods.

outcome.observation: An innovative synthetic route prepared the A-FABP inhibitor (139d) in just two steps, achieving a 50% overall yield (nearly doubling the yield compared to traditional methods).

evidence.text_span: Finally, the A-FABP inhibitor 67,68 (139d) acts on a therapeutic target with dual mechanisms of action that regulate fatty acid transport and suppress inflammatory responses. However, its traditional synthetic methods require six laborious steps: hydrolysis, diazotization, reduction, cyclization, esterification, and hydrolysis. In contrast, an innovative synthetic route provided herein could prepare the target molecule in just two steps: initial construction of the indole core via decarboxylation or alkane activation, followed by benzyl protection/hydrolysis to afford the final product. This streamlined protocol achieves a 50% overall yield—nearly doubling the yield compared to traditional methods—while eliminating hazardous diazotization reactions and multiple purification steps, thereby significantly enhancing both synthetic efficiency and operational safety.

thought.background: The total synthesis of Iprindole and Melatonin has been successfully achieved at small scale.
thought.gap: The scalability and process robustness of these syntheses for industrial production need to be demonstrated.
thought.decision: To demonstrate industrial potential, we attempted and smoothly accomplished decagram-scale production of both Iprindole and Melatonin, expecting scalability and process robustness with minimal yield variation.

outcome.observation: Decagram-scale production of Iprindole was smoothly accomplished, yielding 73.3%, exhibiting scalability and process robustness with minimal yield variation compared to small-scale reactions.

evidence.text_span: To further demonstrate the application potential for this methodology, we attempted and smoothly accomplished decagram-scale production of both Iprindole (73.3%) and Melatonin (54.7%), exhibiting scalability and process robustness with minimal yield variation compared to small-scale reactions.

thought.background: The total synthesis of Iprindole and Melatonin has been successfully achieved at small scale.
thought.gap: The scalability and process robustness of these syntheses for industrial production need to be demonstrated.
thought.decision: To demonstrate industrial potential, we attempted and smoothly accomplished decagram-scale production of both Iprindole and Melatonin, expecting scalability and process robustness with minimal yield variation.

outcome.observation: Decagram-scale production of Melatonin was smoothly accomplished, yielding 54.7%, exhibiting scalability and process robustness with minimal yield variation compared to small-scale reactions.

evidence.text_span: To further demonstrate the application potential for this methodology, we attempted and smoothly accomplished decagram-scale production of both Iprindole (73.3%) and Melatonin (54.7%), exhibiting scalability and process robustness with minimal yield variation compared to small-scale reactions.

thought.background: The bifunctional iron-catalyzed indolization pathway is proposed to involve photoinduced LMCT and Lewis acid-driven mechanisms.
thought.gap: Mechanistic insights into the radical generation and cyclization steps are needed to support the proposed mechanism.
thought.decision: To gain mechanistic insights, we conducted in situ electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) experiments in the presence of DMPO as a spin trapping agent, expecting to detect radicals produced in the iron-catalyzed system.

outcome.observation: Irradiation of the 1a/FeCl3·6H2O/DMPO system generated a sextet signal attributable to a carbon-centered radical. Only a ferric signal was observed without light, indicating cyclohexane 1a was activated to a cyclohexyl radical through iron photocatalysis.

evidence.text_span: Firstly, the radicals produced in the iron-catalyzed system were surveyed by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) in the presence of 5,5-dimethyl−1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapping agent. As shown in Fig. 6a, irradiation of the 1a/FeCl3•6H2O/DMPO system generated a sextet signal (AN = 14.51 G, AH = 21.52 G, and g = 2.0053,) which was attributable to a carbon-centered radical69. Only a ferric signal could be observed without light, indicating that cyclohexane 1a was activated to a cyclohexyl radical through iron photocatalysis.

thought.background: EPR studies confirmed the generation of a carbon-centered radical from cyclohexane.
thought.gap: The interaction of the generated cyclohexyl radical with the diazonium salt needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To understand the radical trapping, we added substrate 1b′ (diazonium salt) into the mixture and performed EPR under irradiation, expecting to detect a nitrogen-centered radical, suggesting rapid radical trapping.

outcome.observation: After the addition of substrate 1b′ into this mixture, a triplet signal ascribed to a nitrogen-centered radical was detected instead under irradiation, suggesting the rapid radical-trapping nature between the cyclohexyl radical and 1b′.

evidence.text_span: Furthermore, after the addition of substrate 1b′ into this mixture, a triplet signal (AN = 14.39 G, AH = 14.59 G, and g = 2.0086) ascribed to a nitrogen-centered radical70 was detected instead under irradiation, suggesting the rapid radicaltrapping nature between the cyclohexyl radical and 1b′ (more details see Figs. S3 and S4).

thought.background: EPR studies confirmed carbon-centered and nitrogen-centered radical generation and trapping for the alkane system.
thought.gap: Similar radical generation and capturing processes need to be confirmed for the carboxylic acid system.
thought.decision: To confirm similar radical processes, we performed EPR with cyclohexanecarboxylic acid 1a′ as substrate, assisted by L1 and DABCO, expecting to observe both carbon-centered and nitrogen-centered radicals via the photoinduced LMCT pathway.

outcome.observation: Assisted by L1 and DABCO, the same carbon-centered and the following nitrogen-centered radicals were both acquired via photoinduced LMCT pathway with cyclohexanecarboxylic acid 1a′ as substrate, demonstrating similar radical generation and capturing processes as 1a.

evidence.text_span: Assisted by L1 and DABCO, the same carboncentered and the following nitrogen-centered radicals were both acquired via photoinduced LMCT pathway with cyclohexanecarboxylic acid 1a′ as substrate, demonstrating similar radical generation and capturing processes as 1a (more details see Figs. S5 and S6).

thought.background: EPR studies indicated radical generation in both alkane and carboxylic acid systems.
thought.gap: Further evidence for radical-mediated C(sp3)–H activation/decarboxylation steps is needed.
thought.decision: To provide further evidence, we conducted radical trapping experiments using superstoichiometric TEMPO, expecting to produce and verify adducts of carbon radicals and TEMPO by GC-MS analysis.

outcome.observation: Radical trapping experiments using superstoichiometric TEMPO produced adducts of carbon radicals and TEMPO, verified by GC-MS analysis, further indicating the achievement of radical-mediated C(sp3)–H activation/decarboxylation steps.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent radical trapping experiments using superstoichiometric 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) produced the adducts of carbon radicals and TEMPO and were verified by GC-MS analysis, further indicating the achievement of radical-mediated C(sp3)–H activation/decarboxylation steps (Fig. 6b, more details see Figs. S1 and S2).

thought.background: Radical generation and trapping have been confirmed.
thought.gap: Insights into the C(sp3)–H cleavage step, specifically whether it is the rate-determining step, are needed.
thought.decision: To gain insights into the C(sp3)–H cleavage step, we carried out kinetic isotopic effect (KIE) studies with separate kinetic experiments using 1a/1a-d12 and a 1:1 mixture of 1a and 1a-d12, expecting to determine KIE values and identify the rate-determining step.

outcome.observation: KIE values of 3.10 (KH/KD) and 3.19 (PH/PD) were measured, implying one of the alkyl C(sp3)–H activation processes to be the 'rate-determining step' for this system.

evidence.text_span: Kinetic isotopic effect (KIE) studies with separate kinetic experiments were next carried out to gain insights into the C(sp3)–H cleavage step (Fig. 6c). The KIE values given by KH/KD and PH/PD were measured as 3.10 and 3.19 by using (1) 1a/1a-d12 respectively as substrates or (2) a 1:1 mixture of 1a and 1a-δd12 as substrates to produce 1c (more details see SI, part IV, iii). These results implied one of the alkyl C(sp3 )–H activation processes to be the “rate-determining step” for this system.

thought.background: KIE studies identified C(sp3)–H activation as the rate-determining step.
thought.gap: The contribution of radical chain processes to the indole construction strategy needs to be quantified.
thought.decision: To quantify the contribution, the quantum yield of the model reaction was calculated, expecting to reflect the extent of radical chain processes.

outcome.observation: The quantum yield of the model reaction was calculated as 0.019, reflecting that radical chain processes made little contribution in the indole construction strategy.

evidence.text_span: Moreover, the quantum yield of the model reaction was calculated as 0.019 (more details see SI, part IV, vii), reflecting that radical chain processes made little contribution in the indole construction strategy.7

thought.background: The mechanism involves in situ diazotization (aniline/tert-butyl nitrite/HBF4) and iron catalysis.
thought.gap: The dual role of iron beyond photocatalysis, specifically as a Lewis acid catalyst for cyclization, needs to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To elucidate the mechanism of cyclization and confirm iron's dual role, we replaced the in situ diazotization system with pre-formed diazonium salt 1b′ and performed the reaction using only FeCl3·6H2O as catalyst without Brønsted acid additives, expecting efficient indole synthesis.

outcome.observation: Initial experiments replacing the in situ diazotization system with pre-formed diazonium salt 1b′ demonstrated efficient indole synthesis using only FeCl3·6H2O as the catalyst without Brønsted acid additives. This preliminarily confirmed iron’s dual role beyond photocatalysis: as a Lewis acid catalyst for cyclization.

evidence.text_span: Initial experiments replacing the in situ diazotization system (aniline/tert-butyl nitrite /HBF4,) with pre-formed diazonium salt 1b′ demonstrated efficient indole synthesis using only FeCl3•6H2O as the catalyst without Brønsted acid additives (Fig. 6d-1). This preliminarily confirmed iron’s dual role beyond photocatalysis: as a Lewis acid catalyst for cyclization.

thought.background: Iron's dual role as a Lewis acid catalyst for cyclization has been preliminarily confirmed.
thought.gap: The role of putative azo intermediates in indole construction needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To further investigate, we substituted diazonium salts with putative azo intermediates (1g) and performed the reaction under iron-mediated Lewis acid catalysis, expecting smooth indole construction with negligible dependence on cyclohexane presence.

outcome.observation: Subsequent substitution of diazonium salts with putative azo intermediates (1g) proceeded smoothly for indole construction under iron-mediated Lewis acid catalysis, with negligible dependence on cyclohexane presence.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent substitution of diazonium salts with putative azo intermediates (1g) proceeded smoothly for indole construction under ironmediated Lewis acid catalysis, with negligible dependence on cyclohexane presence (Fig. 6d-2, 6d-3).

thought.background: Azo intermediates are involved in indole construction.
thought.gap: The role of hydrazone isomer (double-bond migrated derivative of azo intermediate) in indolization needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To further understand the cyclization mechanism, we investigated the reaction with hydrazone isomer 1h (double-bond migrated derivative of 1g), expecting it to also afford the indolization product.

outcome.observation: Further investigation with hydrazone isomer 1h (double-bond migrated derivative of 1g) also afforded the indolization product in an excellent yield (87%).

evidence.text_span: Further investigation with hydrazone isomer 1h (double-bond migrated derivative of 1g) also afforded the indolization product in an excellent yield (87%) (Fig. 6d-4).

thought.background: Iron's dual catalytic functionality (photocatalytic C(sp3)–H activation and Lewis acid-driven sigmatropic rearrangement cyclization) has been demonstrated for the alkane system.
thought.gap: Parallel conclusions need to be substantiated when employing carboxylic acids as alternative substrates.
thought.decision: To confirm iron's dual catalytic functionality for carboxylic acids, we performed analogous control experiments, expecting to confirm simultaneous photocatalytic decarboxylation and Lewis acid-mediated indolization.

outcome.observation: Parallel conclusions were substantiated when employing carboxylic acids as alternative substrates to alkanes. Through analogous control experiments, we confirmed iron’s dual catalytic functionality in simultaneously enabling photocatalytic decarboxylation and Lewis acid-mediated indolization.

evidence.text_span: Building upon these findings, parallel conclusions were substantiated when employing carboxylic acids as alternative substrates to alkanes (Fig. 6e). Through analogous control experiments, we confirmed iron’s dual catalytic functionality in simultaneously enabling photocatalytic decarboxylation and Lewis acid-mediated indolization.

thought.background: Iron's dual catalytic functionality has been confirmed for both alkane and carboxylic acid systems. The acidic (HBF4, KHSO4) and alkaline (DABCO) additives are present in the system.
thought.gap: The role of these additives in the cyclization stage needs to be elucidated, specifically whether they participate in the cyclization.
thought.decision: To elucidate the role of additives, we performed control experiments, expecting to exclude their participation in the cyclization stage.

outcome.observation: The acidic (HBF4 and KHSO4) or alkaline (DABCO) additives present in the system were excluded from participating in the cyclization stage.

evidence.text_span: Importantly, the acidic (·HBF4 and KHSO4.) or alkaline (DABCO) additives present in the system were excluded from participating in the cyclization stage.

thought.background: The role of additives in cyclization has been excluded. A pre-synthesized Fe(III)-carboxylate complex [Fe]-1 was prepared.
thought.gap: The role of additives in the decarboxylation regime needs to be further elucidated, and the nature of the photosensitizer needs to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To elucidate the role of additives and confirm the photosensitizer, we performed spectroscopic analysis of complex [Fe]-1, expecting to reveal its absorption properties and identify it as the putative photosensitizer for alkyl radical generation via LMCT.

outcome.observation: Spectroscopic analysis revealed that complex [Fe]-1 exhibits strong absorption at 405 nm, identifying it as the putative photosensitizer responsible for generating alkyl radicals via an LMCT process.

evidence.text_span: To further elucidate the role of these additives in the decarboxylation regime, we conducted subsequent mechanistic investigations with a pre-synthesized Fe(III)-carboxylate complex [Fe]-1 through Guerinot ’s method72. As presented in Fig. 6f-1, spectroscopic analysis revealed that complex [Fe]−1 exhibits strong absorption at 405 nm, identifying it as the putative photosensitizer responsible for generating alkyl radicals via a LMCT process (more details see Figs. S8 and S9).

thought.background: Complex [Fe]-1 is identified as the photosensitizer for alkyl radical generation. DABCO is present in the system.
thought.gap: The effect of DABCO on iron's Lewis acidity and the cyclization step needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To investigate the effect of DABCO, we reacted [Fe]-1 with diazonium salt 1b′, expecting to observe the formation of the radical-trapped azo product 1g and to see if DABCO masks iron's Lewis acidity, inhibiting cyclization.

outcome.observation: Reaction of [Fe]-1 with diazonium salt 1b′ afforded the radical-trapped azo product 1g in a moderate yield. In this system, stoichiometric Lewis base DABCO masked iron’s Lewis acidity, almost inhibiting the cyclization step.

evidence.text_span: Reaction of [Fe]−1 with diazonium salt 1b′ afforded the radical-trapped azo product 1g in a moderate yield (Fig. 6f-2). In this system, stoichiometric Lewis base DABCO masks iron’s Lewis acidity, almost inhibiting the cyclization step.

thought.background: DABCO masks iron's Lewis acidity, inhibiting cyclization. KHSO4 is also present.
thought.gap: The effect of KHSO4 on cyclization under these conditions needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To investigate the effect of KHSO4, we performed the reaction with [Fe]-1 and diazonium salt 1b′ in the presence of excess Brønsted acid KHSO4, expecting to see if it promotes cyclization.

outcome.observation: Even excess Brønsted acid KHSO4 failed to promote cyclization under these conditions.

evidence.text_span: Notably, even excess Brønsted acid KHSO4 failed to promote cyclization under these conditions.

thought.background: DABCO masks iron's Lewis acidity, inhibiting cyclization, and KHSO4 also failed to promote it.
thought.gap: The effect of removing DABCO on iron's Lewis acidity and indolization efficiency needs to be investigated.
thought.decision: To restore iron's Lewis acidity and enhance indolization, we removed DABCO from the reaction, expecting to observe an increase in indolization efficiency.

outcome.observation: Removal of DABCO partially restored iron’s Lewis acidity, resulting in enhanced indolization efficiency.

evidence.text_span: Removal of DABCO partially restored iron’s Lewis acidity, resulting in enhanced indolization efficiency (Fig. 6f-3).

thought.background: Removal of DABCO partially restored iron's Lewis acidity, enhancing indolization efficiency. The intermediate 1g is formed.
thought.gap: The role of FeCl3·6H2O as an exogenous Lewis acid catalyst in converting intermediate 1g to the target indole product needs to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To confirm the role of FeCl3·6H2O, we supplemented the reaction with FeCl3·6H2O as an exogenous Lewis acid catalyst, expecting to quantitatively convert intermediate 1g into the target indole product.

outcome.observation: Further supplementation with FeCl3·6H2O as an exogenous Lewis acid catalyst quantitatively converted intermediate 1g into the target indole product.

evidence.text_span: Further supplementation with FeCl3•6H2O as an exogenous Lewis acid catalyst quantitatively converted intermediate 1g into the target indole product (Fig. 6f-4).

Overall Yield.interpretation: Highest yield achieved for model reaction 1c.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: For total synthesis of Iprindole, Mebhydrolin, and A-FABP inhibitor.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts during optimization.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The method offers significantly shortened steps and enhanced efficiency compared to conventional approaches for pharmacologically pivotal indole intermediates. For example, Iprindole synthesis was reduced from four steps (43% overall yield) to two steps (75% isolated yield). Mebhydrolin synthesis was improved from four steps (47% overall yield) to a one-pot protocol (56% total yield). Melatonin was constructed in one step (57% yield) compared to multi-step procedures. A-FABP inhibitor synthesis was reduced from six laborious steps to two steps (50% overall yield, nearly doubling the yield).
validation.significance: This work provides a sustainable and industrially viable approach to indole synthesis by integrating iron photocatalysis and Lewis acid catalysis. It enables direct, single-step/one-pot two-step construction of high-value-added indole scaffolds from inexpensive and widely available anilines and alkanes/carboxylic acids, characterized by ease of operation, mild reaction conditions, high efficiency, low cost, and broad substrate applicability. The method's scalability and robustness, demonstrated by decagram-scale production, highlight its potential for industrial manufacturing of active pharmaceutical ingredients.

source.title: Photo-driven bifunctional iron-catalyzed one-pot assembling of indoles from arylamines and alkanes/carboxylic acids.
source.authors: Wang L, Liu S, Zi H, Xu R, Qu F, He W, Gan Z, Gao J, Jin Y.
